title: The New Metabolic World Order and the Ecological Cold War
content:
At the recent World Economic Forum in Davos, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney delivered a sobering message to global leaders. He did not come to offer hope, but rather to pronounce the death of the liberal international order. This elaborate architecture of institutions and norms, constructed after World War II, is now irreversibly broken. However, Carney's precise eulogy arguably understated the true depth of the current global rupture. We are witnessing the end of the fossil-fueled industrial model that made the old order possible in the first place.

The decline of fossil fuels' global hegemony was initially instigated by the manifest instability caused by climate change. It was further accelerated by rivalrous oil-access impediments and ongoing geopolitical conflicts in the Middle East. Unfortunately, instead of a smooth transition to renewable energy, we are now facing fierce, rivalrous competition. The replacement for the liberal international order will not be peacefully negotiated in Geneva or The Hague. Instead, it will be determined by who controls the essential energy flows and critical mineral deposits of the future.

This emerging geopolitical struggle can be best described as an ecological cold war. Unlike the original Cold War, which was a theoretical contest between capitalism and communism, this is a physical battle between competing metabolisms. Middle powers are currently having a crucial moment, acting as strategic crossroads in this new metabolic world order. To successfully navigate this transition, the democratic bloc will need to oppose traditional petrostates by forming a unified 'Green Entente.' Ultimately, this monumental shift will require vast resources, technological innovation, and unprecedented diplomatic acumen.
